parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 1–13

passage.text: The kākāpō
The kākāpō is a nocturnal, flightless parrot that is critically endangered and one of New Zealand’s unique treasures

The kākāpō, also known as the owl parrot, is a large, forest-dwelling bird, with a pale owl-like face. Up to 64 cm in length, it has predominantly yellow-green feathers, forward-facing eyes, a large grey beak, large blue feet, and relatively short wings and tail. It is the world’s only flightless parrot, and is also possibly one of the world’s longest-living birds, with a reported lifespan of up to 100 years.

Kākāpō are solitary birds and tend to occupy the same home range for many years. They forage on the ground and climb high into trees. They often leap from trees and flap their wings, but at best manage a controlled descent to the ground. They are entirely vegetarian, with their diet including the leaves, roots and bark of trees as well as bulbs, and fern fronds.

Kākāpō breed in summer and autumn, but only in years when food is plentiful. Males play no part in incubation or chick-rearing – females alone incubate eggs and feed the chicks. The 1–4 eggs are laid in soil, which is repeatedly turned over before and during incubation. The female kākāpō has to spend long periods away from the nest searching for food, which leaves the unattended eggs and chicks particularly vulnerable to predators.

Before humans arrived, kākāpō were common throughout New Zealand’s forests. However, this all changed with the arrival of the first Polynesian settlers about 700 years ago. For the early settlers, the flightless kākāpō was easy prey. They ate its meat and used its feathers to make soft cloaks. With them came the Polynesian dog and rat, which also preyed on kākāpō. By the time European colonisers arrived in the early 1800s, kākāpō had become confined to the central North Island and forested parts of the South Island. The fall in kākāpō numbers was accelerated by European colonisation. A great deal of habitat was lost through forest clearance, and introduced species such as deer depleted the remaining forests of food. Other predators such as cats, stoats and two more species of rat were also introduced. The kākāpō were in serious trouble.

In 1894, the New Zealand government launched its first attempt to save the kākāpō. Conservationist Richard Henry led an effort to relocate several hundred of the birds to predator-free Resolution Island in Fiordland. Unfortunately, the island didn’t remain predator free – stoats arrived within six years, eventually destroying the kākāpō population. By the mid-1900s, the kākāpō was practically a lost species. Only a few clung to life in the most isolated parts of New Zealand.

From 1949 to 1973, the newly formed New Zealand Wildlife Service made over 60 expeditions to find kākāpō, focusing mainly on Fiordland. Six were caught, but there were no females amongst them and all but one died within a few months of captivity. In 1974, a new initiative was launched, and by 1977, 18 more kākāpō were found in Fiordland. However, there were still no females. In 1977, a large population of males was spotted in Rakiura – a large island free from stoats, ferrets and weasels. There were about 200 individuals, and in 1980 it was confirmed females were also present. These birds have been the foundation of all subsequent work in managing the species.

Unfortunately, predation by feral cats on Rakiura Island led to a rapid decline in kākāpō numbers. As a result, during 1980-97, the surviving population was evacuated to three island sanctuaries: Codfish Island, Maud Island and Little Barrier Island. However, breeding success was hard to achieve. Rats were found to be a major predator of kākāpō chicks and an insufficient number of chicks survived to offset adult mortality. By 1995, although at least 12 chicks had been produced on the islands, only three had survived. The kākāpō population had dropped to 51 birds. The critical situation prompted an urgent review of kākāpō management in New Zealand.

In 1996, a new Recovery Plan was launched, together with a specialist advisory group called the Kākāpō Scientific and Technical Advisory Committee and a higher amount of funding. Renewed steps were taken to control predators on the three islands. Cats were eradicated from Little Barrier Island in 1980, and possums were eradicated from Codfish Island by 1986. However, the population did not start to increase until rats were removed from all three islands, and the birds were more intensively managed. This involved moving the birds between islands, supplementary feeding of adults and rescuing and hand-raising any failing chicks.

After the first five years of the Recovery Plan, the population was on target. By 2000, five new females had been produced, and the total population had grown to 62 birds. For the first time, there was cautious optimism for the future of kākāpō and by June 2020, a total of 210 birds was recorded.

Today, kākāpō management continues to be guided by the kākāpō Recovery Plan. Its key goals are: minimise the loss of genetic diversity in the kākāpō population, restore or maintain sufficient habitat to accommodate the expected increase in the kākāpō population, and ensure stakeholders continue to be fully engaged in the preservation of the species.

parts.passageHtml:
The kākāpō

The kākāpō


The kākāpō is a nocturnal, flightless parrot that is critically endangered and one of New Zealand’s unique treasures

The kākāpō, also known as the owl parrot, is a large, forest-dwelling bird, with a pale owl-like face. Up to 64 cm in length, it has predominantly yellow-green feathers, forward-facing eyes, a large grey beak, large blue feet, and relatively short wings and tail. questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 1–6

Do the following statements agree with the information given in the Reading Passage?

In boxes 1–6 on your answer sheet, write
TRUE  if the statement agrees with the information
FALSE  if the statement contradicts the information
NOT GIVEN  if there is no information on this
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 1–6Do the following statements agree with the information given in the Reading Passage?In boxes 1–6 on your answer sheet, writeTRUE if the statement agrees with the informationFALSE if the statement contradicts the informationNOT GIVEN if there is no information on this
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: There are other parrots that share the kākāpō's inability to fly.
questions.explanation:
FALSE

Câu hỏi:
“There are other parrots that share the kākāpō's inability to fly.”

Bước 1: xác định keyword

other parrots
inability to fly = flightless

Bước 2: scan đoạn đầu bài.

Bài viết:
“It is the world’s only flightless parrot”.

Phân tích:

the world’s only = duy nhất trên thế giới
flightless parrot = loài vẹt không biết bay

Câu hỏi lại nói “có những loài khác cũng như vậy”.

→ Ý bị đảo ngược hoàn toàn.

Nếu bài viết:

“one of several flightless parrots” → TRUE
Nhưng bài viết:
“only flightless parrot” → FALSE.

questions.prompt: Adult kākāpō produce chicks every year.
questions.explanation:
FALSE

Câu hỏi:
“Adult kākāpō produce chicks every year.”

Keyword:

every year

Bài viết:
“Kākāpō breed in summer and autumn, but only in years when food is plentiful.”

Phân tích từng bước:

breed = sinh sản
but only in years = nhưng chỉ vào những năm…
food is plentiful = thức ăn dồi dào

Điều này nghĩa là:
→ không phải năm nào chúng cũng breeding
→ việc sinh sản phụ thuộc vào nguồn thức ăn.

Câu hỏi dùng “every year” → quá tuyệt đối.

IELTS rất thích bẫy bằng:

always
every year
all
only
never

Chỉ cần bài không support tuyệt đối như vậy thì thường sẽ FALSE.

=> FALSE.

questions.prompt: Adult male kākāpō bring food back to nesting females.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: tìm đoạn nói về male/female roles.

Bài viết:
“Males play no part in incubation or chick-rearing – females alone incubate eggs and feed the chicks.”

Phân tích:

no part = không tham gia chút nào
incubation = ấp trứng
chick-rearing = nuôi con

Câu hỏi nói con đực mang thức ăn về cho con cái.

Nhưng nếu male “play no part”, thì chắc chắn không có chuyện chăm sóc hay hỗ trợ thức ăn.

→ Statement trái ý bài.

=> FALSE.

questions.prompt: The Polynesian rat was a greater threat to the kākāpō than Polynesian settlers.
questions.explanation:
Keyword:

greater threat than = nguy hiểm hơn

Bài viết chỉ nói:

settlers săn kākāpō
rats cũng prey on kākāpō

Nhưng bài KHÔNG hề:

so sánh mức độ nguy hiểm
nói bên nào gây hại nhiều hơn

Đây là bẫy comparison.

Có thông tin:
✓ settlers dangerous
✓ rats dangerous

Không có thông tin:
✗ rats more dangerous than settlers

Rất nhiều bạn thấy có cả hai đối tượng xuất hiện rồi tự suy luận.

IELTS không cho phép tự suy luận ngoài bài.

=> NOT GIVEN.

questions.prompt: Kākāpō were transferred from Rakiura Island to other locations because they were at risk from feral cats.
questions.explanation:
TRUE

Câu hỏi:
“Kākāpō were transferred from Rakiura Island to other locations because they were at risk from feral cats.”

Bài viết:
“predation by feral cats on Rakiura Island led to a rapid decline…”

Sau đó:
“the surviving population was evacuated to three island sanctuaries”

Phân tích:

predation by feral cats = bị mèo hoang săn bắt
led to rapid decline = làm số lượng giảm mạnh
evacuated = di chuyển khẩn cấp sang nơi khác

=> Nguyên nhân chuyển đi chính là mối đe doạ từ feral cats.

Đây là dạng TRUE paraphrase:

transferred = evacuated
at risk from = threatened by predation

=> TRUE.

questions.prompt: One Recovery Plan initiative that helped increase the kākāpō population size was caring for struggling young birds.
questions.explanation:
TRUE

Câu hỏi:
“One Recovery Plan initiative that helped increase the kākāpō population size was caring for struggling young birds.”

Bài viết:
“This involved ... rescuing and hand-raising any failing chicks.”

Phân tích:

initiative = hoạt động trong Recovery Plan
failing chicks = struggling young birds
hand-raising = chăm sóc/nuôi bằng tay

Câu hỏi paraphrase rất mạnh:

caring for = rescuing and hand-raising
young birds = chicks

Đây là kỹ thuật IELTS hay dùng:
thay từ đơn giản bằng cụm chuyên môn hơn.

=> TRUE.

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 7–13

Complete the notes below.

Choose ONE WORD AND/OR A NUMBER from the passage for each answer.
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 7–13Complete the notes below.Choose ONE WORD AND/OR A NUMBER from the passage for each answer.
questionGroups.blockHtml:
New Zealand's kākāpō

A type of parrot:

– diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and 7_______

– nests are created in 8_______ where eggs are laid.

Arrival of Polynesian settlers

– the 9_______ of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

Arrival of European colonisers

– 10_______ were an animal which they introduced that ate the kākāpō's food sources.

Protecting kākāpō

– Richard Henry, a conservationist, tried to protect the kākāpō.

– a definite sighting of female kākāpō on Rakiura Island was reported in the year 11_______

– the Recovery Plan included an increase in 12_______

– a current goal of the Recovery Plan is to maintain the involvement of 13_______ in kākāpō protection.
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
– diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [7]
– nests are created in [8] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of Polynesian settlers**
– the [9] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.
**Arrival of European colonisers**
– [10] were an animal which they introduced that ate the kākāpō's food sources.
**Protecting kākāpō**
– Richard Henry, a conservationist, tried to protect the kākāpō.
– a definite sighting of female kākāpō on Rakiura Island was reported in the year [11]
– the Recovery Plan included an increase in [12]
– a current goal of the Recovery Plan is to maintain the involvement of [13] in kākāpō protection.

questions.prompt: 7 owl parrot
questions.explanation:
Bài viết:
“their diet including the leaves, roots and bark of trees as well as bulbs, and fern fronds.”

Bước làm:

various parts of a tree = leaves, roots, bark
fern fronds đã có sẵn trong đề
cần tìm item còn lại chưa dùng.

=> bulbs.

Lưu ý:
Completion thường paraphrase một phần rồi bỏ trống phần còn lại.

=> bulbs
questions.questionHtml:
New Zealand's kākāpō

A type of parrot:

– diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and 7_______

– nests are created in 8_______ where eggs are laid.

Arrival of Polynesian settlers

– the 9_______ of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

Arrival of European colonisers

– 10_______ were an animal which they introduced that ate the kākāpō's food sources.

Protecting kākāpō

– Richard Henry, a conservationist, tried to protect the kākāpō.

– a definite sighting of female kākāpō on Rakiura Island was reported in the year 11_______

– the Recovery Plan included an increase in 12_______

– a current goal of the Recovery Plan is to maintain the involvement of 13_______ in kākāpō protection.

questions.prompt: 8 New Zealand
questions.explanation:
Câu hỏi:
“nests are created in ___ where eggs are laid.”

Bài:
“The 1–4 eggs are laid in soil”

Phân tích:

laid in soil = đẻ trong đất
nests created in soil = tổ được tạo trong đất

Đây là dạng chuyển active/passive nhẹ thôi.

=> soil.

questions.prompt: 9 settlers
questions.explanation:
feathers

Câu hỏi:
“the ___ of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.”

Bài:
“used its feathers to make soft cloaks.”

Paraphrase:

cloaks = clothes
used to make = were used to make

→ vật liệu được dùng là feathers.

=> feathers.

questions.prompt: 10 colonisation
questions.explanation:
“introduced species such as deer depleted the remaining forests of food.”

Phân tích:

depleted forests of food = làm cạn kiệt nguồn thức ăn
introduced species = loài được đưa vào

Ví dụ cụ thể được nêu là deer.

=> deer.

questions.prompt: 11 year
questions.explanation:
“In 1980 it was confirmed females were also present.”

Phân tích:

confirmed = xác nhận chắc chắn
females were present = có con cái xuất hiện

=> “definite sighting” chính là paraphrase của “confirmed”.

=> 1980

questions.prompt: 12 advisory group
questions.explanation:
Bài:
“a higher amount of funding”

Câu hỏi:
“included an increase in ___”

Paraphrase:

higher amount = increase in

=> funding.

questions.prompt: 13 optimistism
questions.explanation:
Bài:
“ensure stakeholders continue to be fully engaged”

Câu hỏi:
“maintain the involvement of ___”

Paraphrase:

involvement = engagement
maintain involvement = continue to be engaged

=> stakeholders.

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 14–26

passage.text: Reintroducing elms to Britain
Mark Rowe investigates attempts to reintroduce elms to Britain
A.

Around 25 million elms, accounting for 90% of all elm trees in the UK, died during the 1960s and ’70s of Dutch elm disease. In the aftermath, the elm, once so dominant in the British landscape, was largely forgotten. However, there’s now hope the elm may be reintroduced to the countryside of central and southern England. Any reintroduction will start from a very low base. ‘The impact of the disease is difficult to picture if you hadn’t seen what was there before,’ says Matt Elliot of the Woodland Trust. ’You look at old photographs from the 1960s and it’s only then that you realise the impact (elms had) ... They were significant, large trees ... then they were gone.’
B.

The disease is caused by a fungus that blocks the elms’ vascular (water, nutrient and food transport) system, causing branches to wilt and die. A first epidemic, which occurred in the 1920s, gradually died down, but in the ’70s a second epidemic was triggered by shipments of elm from Canada. The wood came in the form of logs destined for boat building and its intact bark was perfect for the elm bark beetles that spread the deadly fungus. This time, the beetles carried a much more virulent strain that destroyed the vast majority of British elms.
C.

Today, elms still exist in the southern English countryside but mostly only in low hedgerows between fields. ’We have millions of small elms in hedgerows but they get targeted by the beetle as soon as they reach a certain size,’ says Karen Russell, co-author of the report ’Where we are with elm’. Once the trunk of the elm reaches 10-15 centimetres or so in diameter, it becomes a perfect size for beetles to lay eggs and for the fungus to take hold. Yet mature specimens have been identified, in counties such as Cambridgeshire, that are hundreds of years old, and have mysteriously escaped the epidemic. The key, Russell says, is to identify and study those trees that have survived and work out why they stood tall when millions of others succumbed. Nevertheless, opportunities are limited as the number of these mature survivors is relatively small. ’What are the reasons for their survival?’ Asks Russell. ’Avoidance, tolerance, resistance? We don’t know where the balance lies between the three. I don’t see how it can be entirely down to luck.’
D.

For centuries, elm ran a close second to oak as the hardwood tree of choice in Britain and was in many instances the most prominent tree in the landscape. Not only was elm common in European forests, it became a key component of birch, ash and hazel woodlands. The use of elm is thought to go back to the Bronze Age, when it was widely used for tools. Elm was also the preferred material for shields and early swords. In the 18th century, it was planted more widely and its wood was used for items such as storage crates and flooring. It was also suitable for items that experienced high levels of impact and was used to build the keel of the 19th-century sailing ship Cutty Sark as well as mining equipment.
E.

Given how ingrained elm is in British culture, it’s unsurprising the tree has many advocates. Amongst them is Peter Bourne of the National Elm Collection in Brighton. ‘I saw Dutch elm disease unfold as a small boy,’ he says. ‘The elm seemed to be part of rural England, but I remember watching trees just lose their leaves and that really stayed with me.’ Today, the city of Brighton’s elms total about 17,000. Local factors appear to have contributed to their survival. Strong winds from the sea make it difficult for the determined elm bark beetle to attack this coastal city’s elm population. However, the situation is precarious. ’The beetles can just march in if we’re not careful, as the threat is right on our doorstep,’ says Bourne.
F.

Any prospect of the elm returning relies heavily on trees being either resistant to, or tolerant of, the disease. This means a widespread reintroduction would involve existing or new hybrid strains derived from resistant, generally non-native elm species. A new generation of seedlings have been bred and tested to see if they can withstand the fungus by cutting a small slit on the bark and injecting a tiny amount of the pathogen. ’The effects are very quick,’ says Russell. ’You return in four to six weeks and trees that are resistant show no symptoms, whereas those that are susceptible show leaf loss and may even have died completely.’
G.

All of this raises questions of social acceptance, acknowledges Russell. ’If we’re putting elm back into the landscape, a small element of it is not native - are we bothered about that?’ For her, the environmental case for reintroducing elm is strong. ’They will host wildlife, which is a good thing.’ Others are more wary. ’On the face of it, it seems like a good idea,’ says Elliot. The problem, he suggests, is that, ’You’re replacing a native species with a horticultural analogue*. You’re effectively cloning.’ There’s also the risk of introducing new diseases. Rather than plant new elms, the Woodland Trust emphasises providing space to those elms that have survived independently. ’Sometimes the best thing you can do is just give nature time to recover ... over time, you might get resistance,’ says Elliot.
horticultural analogue: a cultivated plant species that is genetically similar to an existing species

Reintroducing elms to Britain


Reintroducing elms to Britain

It was also suitable for items that experienced high levels of impact and was used to build the keel of the 19th-century sailing ship Cutty Sark as well as mining equipment.

E.

Given how ingrained elm is in British culture, it’s unsurprising the tree has many advocates. Amongst them is Peter Bourne of the National Elm Collection in Brighton. ‘I saw Dutch elm disease unfold as a small boy,’ he says. ‘The elm seemed to be part of rural England, but I remember watching trees just lose their leaves and that really stayed with me.’ Today, the city of Brighton’s elms total about 17,000. Local factors appear to have contributed to their survival. Strong winds from the sea make it difficult for the determined elm bark beetle to attack this coastal city’s elm population. However, the situation is precarious. ’The beetles can just march in if we’re not careful, as the threat is right on our doorstep,’ says Bourne.

F.

Any prospect of the elm returning relies heavily on trees being either resistant to, or tolerant of, the disease. This means a widespread reintroduction would involve existing or new hybrid strains derived from resistant, generally non-native elm species. A new generation of seedlings have been bred and tested to see if they can withstand the fungus by cutting a small slit on the bark and injecting a tiny amount of the pathogen. ’The effects are very quick,’ says Russell. ’You return in four to six weeks and trees that are resistant show no symptoms, whereas those that are susceptible show leaf loss and may even have died completely.’

G.

All of this raises questions of social acceptance, acknowledges Russell. ’If we’re putting elm back into the landscape, a small element of it is not native - are we bothered about that?’ For her, the environmental case for reintroducing elm is strong. ’They will host wildlife, which is a good thing.’ Others are more wary. ’On the face of it, it seems like a good idea,’ says Elliot. The problem, he suggests, is that, ’You’re replacing a native species with a horticultural analogue*. You’re effectively cloning.’ There’s also the risk of introducing new diseases. Rather than plant new elms, the Woodland Trust emphasises providing space to those elms that have survived independently. ’Sometimes the best thing you can do is just give nature time to recover ... over time, you might get resistance,’ says Elliot.

  • horticultural analogue: a cultivated plant species that is genetically similar to an existing species

Reading Passage has sections A–G. Which section contains the following information?

Write the correct letter, A–G, in boxes 14–18 on your answer sheet.

NB You may use any letter more than once.

List of options

A. Paragraph A

B. Paragraph B

C. Paragraph C

D. Paragraph D

E. Paragraph E

F. Paragraph F

G. Paragraph G

14_______  reference to the research problems that arise from there being only a few surviving large elms

15_______  details of a difference of opinion about the value of reintroducing elms to Britain

17_______  a description of the conditions that have enabled a location in Britain to escape Dutch elm disease

**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: reference to the research problems that arise from there being only a few surviving large elms
questions.explanation:
Keyword:

research problems
only a few surviving large elms

Section C có câu:
“opportunities are limited as the number of these mature survivors is relatively small.”

Phân tích:

mature survivors = những cây elm lớn còn sống sót
opportunities are limited = nghiên cứu bị hạn chế vì số lượng quá ít

Đây chính là “research problems”.

=> C

questions.prompt: details of a difference of opinion about the value of reintroducing elms to Britain
questions.explanation:
Keyword:

difference of opinion
value of reintroducing elms

Section G:

Russell cho rằng nên đưa elm quay lại môi trường
Elliot thì thận trọng hơn và phản đối phần nào.

Một bên:
“environmental case for reintroducing elm is strong”

Bên còn lại:
“You’re replacing a native species with a horticultural analogue.”

=> Có sự bất đồng quan điểm rõ ràng.

=> G.

questions.prompt: reference to how Dutch elm disease was brought into Britain
questions.explanation:
B

Keyword:

how disease was brought into Britain

Section B:
“a second epidemic was triggered by shipments of elm from Canada.”

Phân tích:

shipments from Canada = nguồn đưa bệnh vào Anh
logs destined for boat building carried beetles

=> mô tả trực tiếp cách disease vào Britain.

=> B.

questions.prompt: a description of the conditions that have enabled a location in Britain to escape Dutch elm disease
questions.explanation:
Keyword:

conditions
enabled a location to escape disease

Section E:
“Strong winds from the sea make it difficult for the determined elm bark beetle to attack”

Phân tích:

strong sea winds = điều kiện địa lý đặc biệt
khiến beetles khó tấn công Brighton

=> lý do khu vực này tránh được disease.

=> E.

questions.prompt: reference to the stage at which young elms become vulnerable to Dutch elm disease
questions.explanation:
Keyword:

stage
young elms become vulnerable

Section C:
“Once the trunk ... reaches 10–15 centimetres ... it becomes a perfect size for beetles”

Phân tích:

reaches certain size = thời điểm bắt đầu dễ bị attack
vulnerable stage được mô tả rất rõ.

=> C.

questions.prompt: If a tree gets infected with Dutch elm disease, the damage rapidly becomes visible.
questions.explanation:

“If a tree gets infected ... damage rapidly becomes visible.”

Keyword:

rapidly visible

Đoạn F:
“The effects are very quick”

Sau 4–6 tuần:

resistant trees → không sao
susceptible trees → rụng lá/chết

=> Russell đang nói symptoms xuất hiện rất nhanh.

=>
questions.questionHtml:
List of features

A  Matt Elliot

B  Karen Russell

C  Peter Bourne

19_______ If a tree gets infected with Dutch elm disease, the damage rapidly becomes visible.

20_______ It may be better to wait and see if the mature elms that have survived continue to flourish.

21_______ There must be an explanation for the survival of some mature elms.

22_______ We need to be aware that insects carrying Dutch elm disease are not very far away.

23_______ You understand the effect Dutch elm disease has had when you see evidence of how prominent the tree once was.

questions.prompt: It may be better to wait and see if the mature elms that have survived continue to flourish.
questions.explanation:
Câu hỏi:
“It may be better to wait and see...”

Đoạn G:
“Sometimes the best thing you can do is just give nature time to recover”

Phân tích:

không nên can thiệp quá nhiều
cứ để tự nhiên hồi phục
có thể dần dần tự hình thành resistance

=> ý “wait and see”.

=> A

questions.prompt: There must be an explanation for the survival of some mature elms.
questions.explanation:
Câu hỏi:
“There must be an explanation for the survival...”

Đoạn C:
“I don’t see how it can be entirely down to luck.”

Phân tích:
Russell tin rằng:

cây sống sót không thể chỉ do may mắn
phải có nguyên nhân:
avoidance
tolerance
resistance

=>

questions.prompt: We need to be aware that insects carrying Dutch elm disease are not very far away.
questions.explanation:
Câu hỏi:
“insects ... are not very far away.”

Đoạn E:
“the threat is right on our doorstep”

Paraphrase:

threat = beetles carrying disease
on our doorstep = rất gần

=> Peter Bourne cảnh báo danger đang ở ngay nearby.

=> C

questions.prompt: You understand the effect Dutch elm disease has had when you see evidence of how prominent the tree once was.
questions.explanation:
Câu hỏi:
“You understand the effect ... when you see evidence of how prominent the tree once was.”

Đoạn A:
“You look at old photographs ... and it’s only then that you realise the impact”

Phân tích:

old photographs = evidence
realise the impact = hiểu mức độ ảnh hưởng
once prominent = từng rất phổ biến
=> A

questionGroups.blockHtml:
Uses of a popular tree

For hundreds of years, the only tree that was more popular in Britain than elm was 24_______. Starting in the Bronze Age, many tools were made from elm and people also used it to make weapons. In the 18th century, it was grown to provide wood for boxes and 25_______. Due to its strength, elm was often used for mining equipment and the Cutty Sark's 26_______ was also constructed from elm.
questionGroups.noteContent:
**Uses of a popular tree**
For hundreds of years, the only tree that was more popular in Britain than elm was [24]. Starting in the Bronze Age, many tools were made from elm and people also used it to make weapons. In the 18th century, it was grown to provide wood for boxes and [25]. Due to its strength, elm was often used for mining equipment and the Cutty Sark's [26] was also constructed from elm.

questions.prompt: For hundreds of years, the only tree that was more popular in Britain than elm was
questions.explanation:
oak

Đoạn D:
“elm ran a close second to oak”

→ chỉ đứng sau oak về độ phổ biến.

=> oak.

questions.prompt: Starting in the Bronze Age, many tools were made from elm and people also used it to make weapons. In the 18th century, it was grown to provide wood for boxes and
questions.explanation:
Đoạn D:
“used for items such as storage crates and flooring.”

Câu summary đổi:

storage crates = boxes

=> flooring.

questions.prompt: Due to its strength, elm was often used for mining equipment and the Cutty Sark's  was also constructed from elm.
questions.explanation:
Đoạn D:
“used to build the keel of the ... Cutty Sark”

=> keel.

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 27–40

passage.text: How stress affects our judgement

Some of the most important decisions of our lives occur while we’re feeling stressed and anxious. From medical decisions to financial and professional ones, we are all sometimes required to weigh up information under stressful conditions. But do we become better or worse at processing and using information under such circumstances?

My colleague and I, both neuroscientists, wanted to investigate how the mind operates under stress, so we visited some local fire stations. Firefighters’ workdays vary quite a bit. Some are pretty relaxed; they’ll spend their time washing the truck, cleaning equipment, cooking meals and reading. Other days can be hectic, with numerous life-threatening incidents to attend to; they’ll enter burning homes to rescue trapped residents, and assist with medical emergencies. These ups and downs presented the perfect setting for an experiment on how people’s ability to use information changes when they feel under pressure.
We found that perceived threat acted as a trigger for a stress reaction that made the task of processing information easier for the firefighters - but only as long as it conveyed bad news.

This is how we arrived at these results. We asked the firefighters to estimate their likelihood of experiencing 40 different adverse events in their life, such as being involved in an accident or becoming a victim of card fraud. We then gave them either good news (that their likelihood of experiencing these events was lower than they’d thought) or bad news (that it was higher) and asked them to provide new estimates.

People are normally quite optimistic - they will ignore bad news and embrace the good. This is what happened when the firefighters were relaxed; but when they were under stress, a different pattern emerged. Under these conditions, they became hyper-vigilant to bad news, even when it had nothing to do with their job (such as learning that the likelihood of card fraud was higher than they’d thought), and altered their beliefs in response. In contrast, stress didn’t change how they responded to good news (such as learning that the likelihood of card fraud was lower than they’d thought).

Back in our lab, we observed the same pattern in students who were told they had to give a surprise public speech, which would be judged by a panel, recorded and posted online. Sure enough, their cortisol levels spiked, their heart rates went up and they suddenly became better at processing unrelated, yet alarming, information about rates of disease and violence.

When we experience stressful events, a physiological change is triggered that causes us to take in warnings and focus on what might go wrong. Brain imaging reveals that this ’switch’ is related to a sudden boost in a neural signal important for learning, specifically in response to unexpected warning signs, such as faces expressing fear.

Such neural engineering could have helped prehistoric humans to survive. When our ancestors found themselves surrounded by hungry animals, they would have benefited from an increased ability to learn about hazards. In a safe environment, however, it would have been wasteful to be on high alert constantly. So, a neural switch that automatically increases or decreases our ability to process warnings in response to changes in our environment could have been useful. In fact, people with clinical depression and anxiety seem unable to switch away from a state in which they absorb all the negative messages around them.

It is also important to realise that stress travels rapidly from one person to the next. If a co-worker is stressed, we are more likely to tense up and feel stressed ourselves. We don’t even need to be in the same room with someone for their emotions to influence our behaviour. Studies show that if we observe positive feeds on social media, such as images of a pink sunset, we are more likely to post uplifting messages ourselves. If we observe negative posts, such as complaints about a long queue at the coffee shop, we will in turn create more negative posts.

In some ways, many of us now live as if we are in danger, constantly ready to tackle demanding emails and text messages, and respond to news alerts and comments on social media. Repeatedly checking your phone, according to a survey conducted by the American Psychological Association, is related to stress. In other words, a pre-programmed physiological reaction, which evolution has equipped us with to help us avoid famished predators, is now being triggered by an online post. Social media posting, according to one study, raises your pulse, makes you sweat, and enlarges your pupils more than most daily activities.

The fact that stress increases the likelihood that we will focus more on alarming messages, together with the fact that it spreads extremely rapidly, can create collective fear that is not always justified. After a stressful public event, such as a natural disaster or major financial crash, there is often a wave of alarming information in traditional and social media, which individuals become very aware of. But that has the effect of exaggerating existing danger. And so, a reliable pattern emerges - stress is triggered, spreading from one person to the next, which temporarily enhances the likelihood that people will take in negative reports, which increases stress further. As a result, trips are cancelled, even if the disaster took place across the globe; stocks are sold, even when holding on is the best thing to do.

The good news, however, is that positive emotions, such as hope, are contagious too, and are powerful in inducing people to act to find solutions. Being aware of the close relationship between people’s emotional state and how they process information can help us frame our messages more effectively and become conscientious agents of change.

parts.passageHtml:
How stress affects our judgement



Some of the most important decisions of our lives occur while we’re feeling stressed and anxious. From medical decisions to financial and professional ones, we are all sometimes required to weigh up information under stressful conditions. But do we become better or worse at processing and using information under such circumstances?

My colleague and I, both neuroscientists, wanted to investigate how the mind operates under stress, so we visited some local fire stations. Firefighters’ workdays vary quite a bit. Some are pretty relaxed; they’ll spend their time washing the truck, cleaning equipment, cooking meals and reading. Other days can be hectic, with numerous life-threatening incidents to attend to; they’ll enter burning homes to rescue trapped residents, and assist with medical emergencies. These ups and downs presented the perfect setting for an experiment on how people’s ability to use information changes when they feel under pressure.

We then gave them either good news (that their likelihood of experiencing these events was lower than they’d thought) or bad news (that it was higher) and asked them to provide new estimates.

This is what happened when the firefighters were relaxed; but when they were under stress, a different pattern emerged. In contrast, stress didn’t change how they responded to good news (such as learning that the likelihood of card fraud was lower than they’d thought).

When our ancestors found themselves surrounded by hungry animals, they would have benefited from an increased ability to learn about hazards. And so, a reliable pattern emerges - stress is triggered, spreading from one person to the next, which temporarily enhances the likelihood that people will take in negative reports, which increases stress further. The good news, however, is that positive emotions, such as hope, are contagious too, and are powerful in inducing people to act to find solutions. Being aware of the close relationship between people’s emotional state and how they process information can help us frame our messages more effectively and become conscientious agents of change.

questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: In the first paragraph, the writer introduces the topic of the text by
questions.explanation:
C — mentioning a challenge faced by everyone

Câu hỏi:
“In the first paragraph, the writer introduces the topic by…”

Bước 1: đọc câu đầu và xác định main idea.

Đoạn 1:
“Some of the most important decisions of our lives occur while we’re feeling stressed and anxious.”

Ngay sau đó:
“we are all sometimes required to weigh up information under stressful conditions.”

Keyword cực mạnh:

our lives
we are all

=> tác giả đang nói đây là tình huống/challenge mà ai cũng gặp.

Phân tích từng đáp án:

A. defining terms
→ sai, không có định nghĩa stress/anxiety gì cả.

B. questioning an assumption
→ sai, chưa phản bác niềm tin phổ biến nào.

C. mentioning a challenge faced by everyone
→ đúng hoàn toàn.

D. specifying a situation which makes us most anxious
→ sai vì tác giả không chỉ 1 tình huống cụ thể nào “most anxious”.

=> C.

options.label: mentioning a challenge faced by everyone.
specifying a situation which makes us most anxious.

questions.prompt: What point does the writer make about firefighters in the second paragraph?
questions.explanation:
A — regular changes in stress levels make them ideal subjects

Đoạn 2 rất quan trọng.

Tác giả mô tả:

có ngày firefighters rất chill:
“washing the truck, cooking meals…”
có ngày cực stress:
“life-threatening incidents”

Sau đó câu quyết định:
“These ups and downs presented the perfect setting for an experiment”

Phân tích:

ups and downs = lúc stress lúc không
perfect setting = môi trường nghiên cứu lý tưởng

=> chính sự thay đổi stress liên tục khiến firefighters trở thành đối tượng hoàn hảo.

Sai ở các đáp án khác:

B. strategies they use
→ bài không nghiên cứu coping strategies.

C. typical of professions
→ không hề so sánh nghề khác.

D. personalities
→ không nói tính cách.

=> A.

options.label: The regular changes of stress levels in their working lives make them ideal study subjects.
The strategies they use to handle stress are of particular interest to researchers.
The stressful nature of their job is typical of many public service professions.
Their personalities make them especially well-suited to working under stress.

questions.prompt: What is the writer doing in the fourth  paragraph?
questions.explanation:
D — describing methodology

Câu hỏi:
“What is the writer doing in the fourth paragraph?”

Đoạn 4 bắt đầu:
“This is how we arrived at these results.”

Sau đó tác giả kể từng bước:

firefighters estimate risks
researchers give good/bad news
firefighters estimate again

=> đây là quy trình làm thí nghiệm.

Phân biệt các đáp án:

A. explaining findings
→ findings là kết quả, thường kiểu “we found that…”

B. justifying approach
→ sẽ kiểu “we chose firefighters because…”

C. objectives
→ mục tiêu nghiên cứu thường ở đầu.

D. methodology
→ cách tiến hành nghiên cứu.

=> D.

questions.prompt: In the seventh paragraph, the writer describes a mechanism in the brain which
questions.explanation:
C — heightened sensitivity to threats

Câu hỏi:
mechanism in the brain which…

Đoạn 7:
“focus on what might go wrong”

“response to unexpected warning signs”

Phân tích:

warning signs
fear
threats

=> cơ chế não làm con người nhạy hơn với danger signals.

Đáp án C:
“produces heightened sensitivity to indications of external threats”

Paraphrase:

warning signs = indications of threats
focus more strongly = heightened sensitivity

=> C.

options.label: enables people to respond more quickly to stressful situations.
results in increased ability to control our levels of anxiety.
produces heightened sensitivity to indications of external threats.
is activated when there is a need to communicate a sense of danger.

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 31–35

Complete each sentence with the correct ending, A–G, below.

Write the correct letter in boxes 31–35 on your answer sheet.

listOptions.label: took relatively little notice of bad news.
responded to negative and positive information in the same way.
put them in a stressful situation.
behaved in a similar manner, regardless of the circumstances.
thought it more likely that they would experience something bad.

questionGroups.instruction: Questions 31–35Complete each sentence with the correct ending, A–G, below.Write the correct letter in boxes 31–35 on your answer sheet.
questionGroups.blockHtml:
At times when they were relaxed, the firefighters usually 31_______

The researchers noted that when the firefighters were stressed, they 32_______

When the firefighters were told good news, they always 33_______

The students' cortisol levels and heart rates were affected when the researchers 34_______

In both experiments, negative information was processed better when the subjects 35_______

List of options

A. made them feel optimistic.

B. took relatively little notice of bad news.

C. responded to negative and positive information in the same way.

D. were feeling under stress.

E. put them in a stressful situation.

F. behaved in a similar manner, regardless of the circumstances.

G. thought it more likely that they would experience something bad.
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: At times when they were relaxed, the firefighters usually
questions.explanation:
ignored bad news and accepted good news

Câu hỏi:
“At times when they were relaxed…”

Đoạn 5:
“People are normally quite optimistic - they will ignore bad news and embrace the good.”

Ngay sau đó:
“This is what happened when the firefighters were relaxed”

=> relaxed firefighters behaved like normal optimistic humans.

Paraphrase:

embrace good = accept good
ignore bad = ignore bad

options.label: took relatively little notice of bad news.
responded to negative and positive information in the same way.
put them in a stressful situation.
behaved in a similar manner, regardless of the circumstances.
thought it more likely that they would experience something bad.

D. were feeling under stress.

E. put them in a stressful situation.

F. behaved in a similar manner, regardless of the circumstances.

G. thought it more likely that they would experience something bad.

questions.prompt: The researchers noted that when the firefighters were stressed, they
questions.explanation:
became highly alert to bad news

Câu hỏi:
“when firefighters were stressed…”

Đoạn 5:
“they became hyper-vigilant to bad news”
Từ khóa:
hyper-vigilant = cực kỳ cảnh giác/chú ý mạnh
Paraphrase:
highly alert
more sensitive to
=> stressed làm họ absorb bad news mạnh hơn.

options.label: took relatively little notice of bad news.
responded to negative and positive information in the same way.
put them in a stressful situation.
behaved in a similar manner, regardless of the circumstances.
thought it more likely that they would experience something bad.

questions.prompt: When the firefighters were told good news, they always
questions.explanation:
responded the same way

Đoạn 5:
“stress didn’t change how they responded to good news”

Phân tích:

dù stress hay relaxed
reaction với good news không đổi.

=> responded in the same way.

options.label: took relatively little notice of bad news.
responded to negative and positive information in the same way.
put them in a stressful situation.
behaved in a similar manner, regardless of the circumstances.
thought it more likely that they would experience something bad.

questions.prompt: The students' cortisol levels and heart rates were affected when the researchers
questions.explanation:
told students to give a speech

Đoạn 6:
“students who were told they had to give a surprise public speech”

Sau đó:
“their cortisol levels spiked”

Trình tự:

bị yêu cầu speech
stress reaction xảy ra
cortisol + heart rate tăng

=> answer là researchers told them to give a speech.

options.label: took relatively little notice of bad news.
responded to negative and positive information in the same way.
put them in a stressful situation.
behaved in a similar manner, regardless of the circumstances.
thought it more likely that they would experience something bad.

questions.prompt: In both experiments, negative information was processed better when the subjects
questions.explanation:
were under stress

Câu hỏi:
negative information processed better when…

Bài lặp lại idea này nhiều lần:

Đoạn 3:
“processing information easier… bad news”

Đoạn 5:
“under stress… hyper-vigilant to bad news”

Đoạn 6:
students stressed → process alarming info better

=> điểm chung:
subjects were under stress.

options.label: took relatively little notice of bad news.
responded to negative and positive information in the same way.
put them in a stressful situation.
behaved in a similar manner, regardless of the circumstances.
thought it more likely that they would experience something bad.

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 36–40

Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in the Reading Passage?

In boxes 36–40 on your answer sheet, write
YES  if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer
NO  if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer
NOT GIVEN  if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 36–40Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in the Reading Passage?In boxes 36–40 on your answer sheet, writeYES if the statement agrees with the claims of the writerNO if the statement contradicts the claims of the writerNOT GIVEN if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: The tone of the content we post on social media tends to reflect the nature of the posts in our feeds.
questions.explanation:
Câu hỏi:
social media posts reflect what we see in feeds.

Đoạn 9:

positive feeds → uplifting posts
negative feeds → negative posts

Đây là paraphrase trực tiếp:

tone reflects nature of feeds.

=> YES.

questions.prompt: Phones have a greater impact on our stress levels than other electronic media devices.
questions.explanation:
Bài chỉ nói:
“checking your phone ... is related to stress”

Nhưng không có:

comparison với laptops/tablets/TV

Câu hỏi dùng comparative:
“greater impact than”

IELTS rule:
Có mention ≠ có comparison.

=> NOT GIVEN.

questions.prompt: The more we read about a stressful public event on social media, the less able we are to take the information in.
questions.explanation:
NO

Câu hỏi:
“the less able we are to take information in”

Nhưng bài nói ngược lại.

Đoạn cuối:
“people will take in negative reports”

và:
“become very aware”

=> stress làm absorb information mạnh hơn, không phải kém đi.

=> NO.

questions.prompt: Stress created by social media posts can lead us to take unnecessary precautions.
questions.explanation:
YES

Đoạn cuối:
“trips are cancelled”

“stocks are sold”

Ngay cả khi:

disaster ở rất xa
selling stocks là quyết định không hợp lý

=> stress khiến người ta làm unnecessary actions/precautions.

=> YES.

questions.prompt: Our tendency to be affected by other people's moods can be used in a positive way.
questions.explanation:
YES

Đoạn cuối:
“positive emotions ... are contagious too”

“powerful in inducing people to act to find solutions”

=> việc cảm xúc lan truyền có thể được tận dụng tích cực.

=> YES.
